column1: # sheet.calc.decimal.divergence golden corpus — the D-6 exact-decimal SPIKE
# (spec §3, §5.1, M3). Cases where IEEE-754 f64 (the v1 default, `F64` Numeric)
# and an exact base-10 decimal (`Decimal` Numeric, rust_decimal, behind the
# `exact-decimal` feature) produce a DIFFERENT result at the f64 boundary.
# Format: id<TAB>op<TAB>operands<TAB>f64<TAB>decimal<TAB>diverges
#   id        case id (unique within the file).
#   op        the operation the test replays through BOTH Numeric impls:
#               add   — left-fold add over the operand list.
#               binop — a single binary op; operands = "a OP b" (OP in + - * /).
#               accum — add operands[1] to a running total operands[0] times
#                       (e.g. 100;0.01 = add 0.01 a hundred times).
#   operands  ';'-separated f64 literals (the values the USER typed); for binop
#             the form is "a OP b" (space-separated).
#   f64       the EXACT result of the op under `F64` (f64.to_string()), the
#             value Excel itself would carry (Excel is f64-based, D-6).
#   decimal   the EXACT result of the op under `Decimal`, lowered with to_f64()
#             (f64.to_string()) — the clean base-10 value.
#   diverges  "yes" if f64 != decimal at the f64 boundary, "no" if they agree.
# The "no" rows are the honest contrast: f64 is NOT always wrong — sums of
# exactly-representable binary fractions (halves/quarters/eighths) and of
# integers foot exactly, as does this particular invoice. The spike's value is
# the "yes" rows: pennies and tenths that drift in binary but are exact in
# base-10. NOTE: the divergence depends on accumulation ORDER — these goldens
# are a strict LEFT-FOLD from 0.0 (the order the test replays), so ten 0.1s
# fold to 0.9999999999999999, NOT the 1.0 a pairwise/Kahan sum might reach.
# FURTHER NOTE (documented in decimal_spike.rs + DECIMAL-SPIKE.md): 1/3 is
# non-terminating in base-10 TOO, so decimal is not a cure-all — that case is
# asserted separately in the test, not here.
# --- the canonical tenths-drift case (every decimal pitch deck's slide 1) ---
classic-0.1+0.2
three-tenths
one-one-plus-two-two
# --- money: cent columns that must foot exactly ---
money-cents
penny-accumulation
invoice-lines
# --- the tenths-drift over a longer accumulation (left-fold from 0.0) ---
ten-tenths
# --- the honest contrast: f64 is exact for binary-representable fractions ---
exact-halves
integer-sum
